one_line_takeaway: A nine-model red-team audit showing production LLM guardrails are broadly bypassable with simple single-turn jailbreaks -- especially for cyber-offense content -- quantified via a new relative Risk Severity Index on which closed frontier models (GPT-4o, Gemini) score safest and DeepSeek V3 the riskiest.
key_finding: Guardrails are widely and easily bypassed, and are markedly weaker for cybersecurity harms than for safety harms. Closed frontier models were safest but far from robust; open-weight and rapidly-iterated models were severely exposed. Prompts tuned only against DeepSeek transferred effectively to other models, confirming cross-model transferability of jailbreaks.
core_contribution: An empirical safety/security red-team audit of nine production LLMs (Claude Opus 4, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Grok 3, Llama 4 Scout, Mistral 7B, Qwen 3 1.7B, and DeepSeek V3 in both online and open-source variants) against a hand-crafted adversarial prompt dataset spanning 24 harm categories (17 safety, 7 security) grouped into 7 harm topics. The paper's own novel artifact is the Risk Severity Index (RSI), a single composite score defined as RSI = (1 + Defect Rate - Refusal Rate) / 2, bounded in [0,1], used to rank and compare the models' risk posture per harm topic. Note: frameworks such as HarmBench, SAGE, and REVEAL, and prior studies by Jindal et al. and Omar et al., appear only as Related Work / motivation (their Defect/Refusal/SUI metrics inspired RSI) and are NOT contributions or re-run baselines of this paper.
threat_model: A threat actor (ranging from a 'script kiddie' to an experienced adversary) who submits single-turn adversarial prompts to a deployed LLM to elicit harmful content the model's guardrails should refuse: violent-crime and weapons/CBRN instructions, malicious/polymorphic malware and attack payloads, phishing/social-engineering content, disinformation and election-manipulation material, self-harm instructions, illegal sexual content, and insecure code emitted without disclaimers. The attacker relies on lightweight prompt-injection/jailbreak tactics (obscuring intent, feigning ethical/educational use, impersonating authority, 'label this as fictional'). A key assumption stress-tested is transferability: prompts tuned to bypass DeepSeek V3's guardrails are reused verbatim against the other eight models.
limitations: (1) The entire prompt dataset was optimized against DeepSeek V3, which the authors concede puts DeepSeek at an inherent disadvantage and means RSI is explicitly a RELATIVE inter-model index, not a standalone safety score. (2) Single-turn only by design, so multi-turn/crescendo attack surface is not measured. (3) Small scale: aggregate rates rest on ~5 prompts per category with only 2 representative prompts deeply analyzed per category in Section V, so per-topic percentages come from very small denominators (e.g. 40%/73%/93% imply ~15-prompt topic buckets). (4) Human-expert annotation is subjective despite double-checking; no inter-annotator agreement statistic is reported. (5) The RSI weighting constant is stated inconsistently ('0.005 per percentage point' in Sec IV vs '0.05 per percentage point' in Sec VI), and RSI weights defect and refusal equally by fiat without justification. (6) Point-in-time snapshot of guardrails that vendors patch continuously; results are not reproducible against evolving hosted models.

datasets_used: Authors' own publicly released adversarial prompt dataset: 24 harm categories (17 safety, 7 security) across 7 harm topics, ~5 prompts per category, single-turn, designed/refined against DeepSeek V3 Online

Nine target LLMs evaluated: Claude Opus 4 (Anthropic API), GPT-4o (web), Gemini 2.5 Flash (web), Grok 3 (web), Llama 4 Scout (Ollama), Mistral 7B (Ollama), Qwen 3 1.7B (Ollama), DeepSeek V3 Online, DeepSeek V3 Open-Source (Ollama)
quantitative_results: Adversarial dataset was effective against DeepSeek V3 Online in 91% of cases (exceptions: Insecure Code, Bias, Privacy categories)

Refusal Rate ranking: Gemini 2.5 Flash 48% (highest), GPT-4o 42%, Claude Opus 4 27%, Llama 4 Scout 16%, Grok 3 5%, Mistral 7B 5%, DeepSeek V3 Open-Source 4%, Qwen 3 1.7B 3%, DeepSeek V3 Online 2% (lowest)

Defect Rate ranking: GPT-4o 39% and Gemini 2.5 Flash 39% (lowest/safest), Claude Opus 4 54%, Llama 4 Scout 72%, Mistral 7B 76%, Grok 3 82%, DeepSeek V3 Open-Source 86%, Qwen 3 1.7B 87%, DeepSeek V3 Online 90% (highest/riskiest)

Average refusal rate was 22% for safety categories vs only 12% for cybersecurity categories; every model refused less on cyber harms; five models (Grok 3, Qwen 3 1.7B, Mistral 7B, DeepSeek V3 Online and Open-Source) refused ZERO cybersecurity-category prompts
Claude Opus 4 showed the largest safety-vs-security defect-rate gap at 30% (next largest Qwen 3 1.7B at 19%)
Overall average RSI approx 0.800 across the six high-risk harm topics

Highest-risk harm topic: Immoral or Illegal Sexual Deviancy with average RSI 0.870 (highest of any topic); Advancing Societal Harm average RSI 0.597 (second safest); Special Interests (bias, censorship) had the lowest average RSI of any topic

Per-model RSI, Advancing Violent/Terroristic Behavior topic (Table 51): GPT-4o 0.367 (Minor), Gemini 2.5 Flash 0.500 (Moderate), Claude Opus 4 0.633 (Significant), Llama 4 Scout 0.733, Mistral 7B 0.767, Grok 3 0.967, Qwen 3 1.7B 0.967 (Severe), DeepSeek V3 Online 1.000 and Open-Source 1.000 (Maximal Harm Observed)
baselines_compared: No external method/tool baseline is run; the nine LLMs are compared against EACH OTHER on the authors' own RSI, Defect Rate, and Refusal Rate

DeepSeek V3 Online serves as the internal 'baseline' model used to generate and validate the adversarial prompts (91% attack success), against which the other eight models' guardrails are relatively measured

Prior frameworks (HarmBench, SAGE, REVEAL) and studies (Jindal et al., Omar et al., Abdali et al., Beyer et al., Pathade et al., Hasanov et al.) are cited as Related Work in Table 2 for context/metric lineage only -- they are NOT experimental baselines reproduced in this paper
